label: Metric Tensor
definition: The object that defines an inner product in each tangent space of a manifold, supplying the local rule for measuring lengths, angles, and ultimately distances and curvature across a possibly curved space.

body.text: The metric tensor is the object that tells you how to measure lengths and angles at each point of a manifold. Concretely it assigns to every tangent space a smoothly varying inner product, usually represented as a symmetric positive-definite matrix that can change from point to point. Given two tangent vectors at a point, the metric returns a number that plays the role of their dot product, from which the length of a single vector and the angle between two of them follow immediately. A bare manifold knows only its smooth shape; supplying a metric tensor is exactly what upgrades it to a Riemannian manifold and gives it genuine geometry, which is why the metric is often called simply "the metric."

Everything quantitative about a curved space is computed from its metric. To find the length of a path, you integrate the metric-induced speed of its tangent vector along the way; the shortest path between two points is a geodesic, and its length is the geodesic distance. Because the matrix entries can vary across the manifold, the rule for measuring vectors is local: the same coordinate displacement can correspond to a long distance in one region and a short one in another. The way the metric forces nearby geodesics to converge or diverge is precisely what curvature measures, so the metric tensor is the single piece of data from which distance, angle, geodesics, and curvature are all derived. In flat Euclidean space the metric is just the identity matrix everywhere, recovering the ordinary Pythagorean distance and making the flat case the trivial special case of the general theory.

The metric tensor is the lever that lets you choose what distance means, and this is its importance for machine learning. The default Euclidean or cosine comparison of embedding vectors is the choice of a trivial constant metric, but it is a choice, and other metrics encode other notions of similarity. A learned or position-dependent metric, sometimes called a pullback metric when it is induced by how a decoder maps a latent space into data space, reveals that the honest distance between two representations is generally not the naive straight-line one, and that the latent space is best read as a curved Riemannian manifold whose metric the simple distance only approximates. Distance metric learning is, in this language, the explicit fitting of a metric tensor so that semantically similar items come out close.

The most striking example of the metric setting the meaning of distance is the Fisher information metric of information geometry, where the metric tensor on a space of probability distributions makes distance correspond to statistical distinguishability rather than raw parameter difference. Natural gradient descent works by preconditioning with this metric so that optimization steps respect how much the model's output actually moves. Hyperbolic embeddings are another instance, where deliberately adopting a metric of negative curvature gives hierarchies the room they need. In each case the recurring lesson is that geometry is a modeling choice: pick the metric tensor, and you have picked what length, angle, and distance mean for your problem.

image.alt: A globe in orthographic view overlaid with a regular grid of circles that stay round near the center and flatten into ellipses toward the rim.
image.caption: The metric tensor is the local rule for measuring length and angle, and it can change from place to place. A grid of identical circles on the sphere appears round at the center but squashes into ellipses toward the edge, showing how the same true distance is stretched differently depending on position and direction.
